title: Red Wine on Silk
stainChemistry: Red wine anthocyanins and condensed tannin polymers hydrogen-bond aggressively to silk's peptide backbone. Silk is a protein fiber (fibroin) with pH tolerance of 4.0–7.0 — it weakens in alkaline conditions and is destroyed by chlorine bleach and protease enzymes. The alcohol in wine loosens silk dyes, making dye bleeding a primary risk alongside the tannin set.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flush prevents tannin oxidation while avoiding the dye bleeding that warm water accelerates on silk. Acetic acid (white vinegar) protonates the anthocyanin hydroxyl groups, breaking hydrogen bonds to the silk fibroin without pushing pH above silk's safe ceiling. Wine Away's reducing chemistry converts the anthocyanin flavylium cation to its colorless carbinol form — specifically targeting the chromophore without risking the protein fiber. No enzymes, no alkali, no chlorine — all of which destroy silk.

spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle blotting with cold distilled water from back of fabric
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold (60–65°F) — heat sets tannin permanently and accelerates silk dye bleeding
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place silk face-down on clean white towel. Flush from the back with cold distilled water using a spray bottle — never rub silk, the wet fibers are 20% weaker than dry. Blot gently. Check towel for dye transfer before proceeding.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go (diluted 1:3 with cold water for silk)
spottingProtocol.technique: Feather application with cotton swab from outside edge inward
spottingProtocol.temperature: Ambient — no steam on silk during tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dilute Tannin-Go 1:3 for silk safety. Apply with cotton swab from outer edge of stain inward. The mild acidity disrupts tannin-fiber hydrogen bonds without exceeding silk's pH floor. Work gently — no brushing, no spatula on silk. Flush with cold water after 2–3 minutes. Do not let acid dwell longer — prolonged acid contact weakens silk peptide chains.

spottingProtocol.agent: Wine Away Red Wine Stain Remover
spottingProtocol.technique: Apply directly to remaining anthocyanin color, blot with white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: If red/purple color remains after Tannin-Go, apply Wine Away directly. The reducing chemistry converts the anthocyanin flavylium cation to a colorless carbinol form without oxidizer risk. Watch the stain — you should see red fade to pink to clear within 30–90 seconds on fresh stains. Blot gently. This product is silk-safe because it avoids bleach, enzymes, and alkali.

spottingProtocol.agent: Acetic acid rinse (white vinegar diluted 1:4 with cold water)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Spray dilute vinegar solution across treated area to neutralize any residual chemistry and prevent tannin rebound. Flush immediately with cold distilled water. Blot dry between clean white towels — never wring or twist silk. Air dry flat away from direct sunlight. Never apply heat until stain is confirmed gone.

homeSolutions: Fresh spill: Blot immediately with a clean white cloth — never rub silk. Flush with cold water only. Apply white vinegar diluted 1:1 with cold water, let sit 2 minutes, blot and rinse.
Set stain: Apply Wine Away, cover with plastic wrap to keep moist, let sit 15–30 minutes. Rinse with cold water and repeat if needed. Do not use OxiClean or any oxygen bleach on silk.

When to stop: If the stain has turned brown (oxidized tannin) and vinegar/Wine Away aren't lifting it, stop. Take to a professional dry cleaner — home oxidizer options are too harsh for silk. Tell them it's oxidized red wine on silk.
materialWarnings: NEVER use protease enzymes on silk — silk IS a protein fiber (fibroin); protease will digest the fabric itself
NEVER use chlorine bleach on silk — destroys the protein structure and causes irreversible yellowing
NEVER use hot water or steam — sets tannin permanently AND accelerates dye bleeding on silk
NEVER use ammonia or alkaline spotters — silk degrades above pH 7.0; alkali also darkens tannin stains
NEVER rub wet silk — fibroin loses 20% tensile strength when wet, causing fiber breakage and sheen loss

professional.name: A.L. Wilson Tannin-Go
professional.use: Diluted 1:3 for silk — primary tannin bond breaker
professional.note: Acetic acid base disrupts hydrogen bonds between anthocyanin and silk fibroin without alkali risk

professional.name: Wine Away Red Wine Stain Remover
professional.use: Reducing agent for residual anthocyanin chromophore
professional.note: Silk-safe — no bleach, no enzymes, no alkali. Targets the flavylium cation specifically.

professional.name: Street's Tannin Remover
professional.use: Alternative tannin spotter — dilute for silk
professional.note: More concentrated than Tannin-Go; must dilute 1:4 minimum for silk safety

consumer.name: Wine Away Red Wine Stain Remover
consumer.use: Apply directly to red wine stain on silk
consumer.note: One of the few consumer products safe for silk wine stains — reducing chemistry, no bleach

consumer.name: White distilled vinegar (5% acetic acid)
consumer.use: Dilute 1:1 with cold water as tannin spotter
consumer.note: Safe, gentle acid that breaks tannin bonds without damaging silk

consumer.name: Carbona Stain Devils #1 (Coffee, Tea, Wine)
consumer.use: Follow label — test on hidden seam first
consumer.note: Formulated for tannin stains; test carefully on silk for dye safety

escalation.when: Stain has turned brown (oxidized), or Wine Away + Tannin-Go haven't removed it after two cycles, or dye bleeding is visible around stain
escalation.whatToTell: This is an oxidized red wine stain on silk. I did not use heat, alkali, or enzymes. I used cold water, diluted tannin spotter, and Wine Away reducing agent. The anthocyanin has polymerized beyond what home chemistry can reverse.
escalation.specialistType: Dry cleaner with GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent — PERC can leave odor and affect silk hand-feel. Ask for wet-side pre-spotting with professional tannin formula before the cleaning cycle.

solventNote: Pre-spot on wet side with diluted tannin formula before dry cleaning cycle. GreenEarth or hydrocarbon solvent preferred for silk — PERC safe but may affect hand-feel and retain odor. For set stains showing brown oxidation, POG pre-treatment on dry side can help break the polymerized tannin shell before wet-side acid treatment. Avoid hot dryer — air dry or low heat only.
